title: KV Cache
description: KV Cache is the #1 inference optimization in LLMs. It trades memory for compute by storing Key and Value matrices across decoding steps. FAANG interviews test your ability to calculate its size, explain its limits, and describe system-level solutions.

sections.title: Pre-fill vs. Decoding Phase
sections.description:
LLM inference has two distinct phases — understanding both is required for system design interviews.

Pre-fill (Prompt Processing): The full input prompt is processed in parallel through all N transformer layers. This is compute-bound and highly GPU-efficient (like training). Outputs the K and V matrices for every token and layer — this is the initial KV cache population. Also produces the first output token.

Decoding (Auto-regressive Generation): Tokens are generated one at a time. Each step only processes a single new token, using the cached K,V for all previous positions. This is memory-bandwidth bound — the bottleneck is reading the KV cache from VRAM, not arithmetic.

sections.title: The Problem: O(N²) Without Caching
sections.description: Without KV Cache, generating token N requires running attention over all N tokens, recomputing K and V for every previous token at each step. For a sequence of length T, total compute is O(T²) — catastrophically slow for long sequences.
sections.codeBlock:
# Naive generation: recomputes K, V for ALL past tokens every step
def generate_naive(prompt_tokens, model, max_new=100):
    tokens = list(prompt_tokens)
    for step in range(max_new):
        # Recompute attention over ENTIRE sequence — O(T²) total
        x = model.embed(tokens)
        for layer in model.layers:
            Q = layer.W_q(x)    # [T, d]
            K = layer.W_k(x)    # [T, d]  ← wasteful: re-computed
            V = layer.W_v(x)    # [T, d]  ← wasteful: re-computed
            x = layer.attn(Q, K, V) + x
            x = layer.ffn(x) + x
        next_tok = x[-1].argmax()  # only care about last token
        tokens.append(next_tok)
    return tokens
# Cost at step T: O(T) attention ops → total O(T²) for T steps

sections.title: The Solution: KV Cache — O(N) per Step
sections.description:
The Key and Value projections for previous tokens never change — they only depend on the token itself, not on future context (decoder-only models use causal masking). So we store them once and reuse.

At each decoding step: compute Q, K, V only for the new token, append K/V to cache, run attention using full cached K/V. Complexity per step drops from O(T) to O(1) (compute) — limited only by reading the cache from VRAM.
sections.codeBlock:
def decode_with_kv_cache(new_token, past_kv, model):
    """
    past_kv: dict of {'k': Tensor[layers, 1, past_len, d],
                      'v': Tensor[layers, 1, past_len, d]}
    """
    for i, layer in enumerate(model.layers):
        # Only compute for the single new token
        q = layer.W_q(new_token)          # [1, 1, d]
        k_new = layer.W_k(new_token)      # [1, 1, d]
        v_new = layer.W_v(new_token)      # [1, 1, d]

        # Append to cache (growing each step)
        k = torch.cat([past_kv['k'][i], k_new], dim=1)  # [1, T, d]
        v = torch.cat([past_kv['v'][i], v_new], dim=1)
        past_kv['k'][i], past_kv['v'][i] = k, v

        # Attention: Q is [1,1,d], K/V are [1,T,d] — O(T) dot products
        scores = q @ k.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(d_k)
        new_token = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1) @ v
    return new_token, past_kv

sections.title: KV Cache Memory: The Exact Formula (Must Know)
sections.description:
This is a very common FAANG interview question: calculate the KV cache size for a given model.

KV Cache (bytes) = 2 × n_layers × n_kv_heads × head_dim × seq_len × batch_size × bytes_per_param

The factor of 2 is for K and V. For FP16: 2 bytes. For INT8 quantized KV: 1 byte.

Worked example — Llama 3 8B:
• n_layers = 32 · n_kv_heads = 8 · head_dim = 128 · batch=1 · seq=8192 · FP16 (2 bytes)
• = 2 × 32 × 8 × 128 × 8192 × 2 = ~1.07 GB per request
• Serving 50 concurrent users → ~53 GB just for KV cache

Implication: For production serving, KV cache memory often exceeds model weight memory — it drives the GPU fleet sizing.
sections.codeBlock:
def kv_cache_bytes(
    n_layers, n_kv_heads, head_dim,
    seq_len, batch_size, dtype_bytes=2  # FP16
):
    # ponytail: formula assumes full KV heads (MHA). For GQA divide by groups.
    return 2 * n_layers * n_kv_heads * head_dim * seq_len * batch_size * dtype_bytes

# Llama 3 8B
llama3_8b = kv_cache_bytes(
    n_layers=32, n_kv_heads=8, head_dim=128,
    seq_len=8192, batch_size=1
)
print(f'Llama 3 8B @ 8K context: {llama3_8b / 1e9:.2f} GB')  # ~1.07 GB

# Llama 3 70B at 128K context with batch=4
llama3_70b = kv_cache_bytes(
    n_layers=80, n_kv_heads=8, head_dim=128,
    seq_len=131072, batch_size=4
)
print(f'Llama 3 70B @ 128K, batch=4: {llama3_70b / 1e9:.1f} GB')  # ~429 GB

sections.title: MQA vs GQA: Shrinking the KV Cache
sections.description:
The KV cache bottleneck drove two architectural innovations that reduce its size by sharing K/V heads across multiple Q heads.

Multi-Query Attention (MQA): All Q heads share a single K and V head. Reduces KV cache by n_heads× (e.g., 32× for Llama). Risk: accuracy degradation on complex tasks. Used in Falcon, early PaLM.

Grouped-Query Attention (GQA): Q heads divided into G groups; each group shares K/V. GQA(G=H) = MHA, GQA(G=1) = MQA. Best quality/memory tradeoff. Llama 3 (8B: 8 KV heads, 32 Q heads = 4 groups), Mistral, Gemma all use GQA.

ModelQ headsKV headsReduction
GPT-2 (MHA)12121×
Llama 3 8B (GQA)3284×
Llama 3 70B (GQA)6488×
Mistral 7B (GQA)3284×
sections.codeBlock:
# GQA implementation — repeat K/V heads to match Q heads
def gqa_attention(Q, K, V, num_q_heads, num_kv_heads):
    """
    Q: [B, num_q_heads, T_q, d]
    K: [B, num_kv_heads, T_kv, d]
    V: [B, num_kv_heads, T_kv, d]
    """
    groups = num_q_heads // num_kv_heads
    # Expand KV heads: each KV head repeated `groups` times
    K = K.repeat_interleave(groups, dim=1)  # [B, num_q_heads, T_kv, d]
    V = V.repeat_interleave(groups, dim=1)
    # Standard scaled dot-product
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    attn = F.softmax(Q @ K.transpose(-2,-1) / d_k**0.5, dim=-1)
    return attn @ V

# Memory comparison: MHA vs GQA for 1B token sequence
base = 2 * 32 * 128 * 1_000_000 * 2  # MHA, 32 heads
gqa  = 2 *  8 * 128 * 1_000_000 * 2  # GQA, 8 KV heads
print(f'MHA: {base/1e9:.1f}GB | GQA: {gqa/1e9:.1f}GB | Saving: {(base-gqa)/1e9:.1f}GB')

sections.title: FlashAttention: Fused Kernel for Speed
sections.description:
FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) doesn't change the math — it changes where computation happens. Standard attention materializes the full [T, T] attention matrix in HBM (slow VRAM). FlashAttention tiles the computation in SRAM (fast on-chip), never writing the full matrix to HBM.

Results: 2–4× faster attention, 5–20× less peak memory during training. No accuracy change. Used by PyTorch 2.0 natively via F.scaled_dot_product_attention.

FlashAttention-2 (2023): Better parallelism across sequence dimension — 2× over FA1 on A100.
FlashAttention-3 (2024): Targets H100 Tensor Cores with async pipelining.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def flash_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True):
    """
    PyTorch 2.0+ wraps FlashAttention automatically.
    is_causal=True applies the causal mask for decoder-only LLMs.
    No explicit softmax/matmul — kernel handles tiling in SRAM.
    """
    # ponytail: falls back to math kernel if FA not available;
    #           check torch.backends.cuda.flash_sdp_enabled()
    return F.scaled_dot_product_attention(
        Q, K, V,
        attn_mask=None,
        dropout_p=0.0,
        is_causal=is_causal
    )
# vs manual attention (materializes [T,T] in HBM — slow for long T)
def slow_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1) / d_k**0.5  # [B, H, T, T] → HBM
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
    return F.softmax(scores, dim=-1) @ V

sections.title: Context Extension — YaRN, NTK-Aware & Position Interpolation
sections.description:
Models are trained at a specific context length (e.g., Llama 2: 4K, Llama 3: 8K, GPT-4: 32K). To extend beyond training length without retraining, you need to modify the positional encoding. This is a common FAANG system design question.

Position Interpolation (PI): Scale position indices by ratio S/T (S = training length, T = target length). Simple: embeds[t] = emb_original[t × S/T]. Works up to ~8× extension with minimal finetuning. Used by Meta for Llama 2 context extension (4K → 32K).

NTK-Aware Scaling: Instead of scaling positions uniformly, scale the frequencies of RoPE's sinusoidal components by different factors. Low frequencies (long-range) get scaled more, high frequencies (local) less. This preserves the model's ability to distinguish nearby tokens (high-freq detail) while extending range. Formula: base = base_original × α^(d/(d-2)), where α = (T/S)^(1/(d/2-1)). Works up to ~16× extension without fine-tuning in many models.

YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN, Peng et al., 2023): Combines NTK-aware scaling with attention temperature scaling and a ramp function that only modifies higher RoPE dimensions. The ramp ensures the first few dimensions (short-range) are unchanged, and deeper dimensions (long-range) are scaled. Achieves 32× context extension (4K → 128K) with minimal fine-tuning. Used in Together AI's Llama-2-7B-32K.

FAANG interview tip: When asked 'How would you extend a model's context from 8K to 128K?' — propose: (1) Start with YaRN for position encoding (ramp+NTK), (2) Fine-tune for 100-1000 steps on long-context data, (3) Use FlashAttention to avoid OOM during training, (4) Verify 'lost in the middle' degradation with a needle-in-a-haystack test.
sections.codeBlock:
# YaRN — position encoding scaling (simplified)
import torch

def yarn_scale(dim, max_position, base=10000.0, scale=8.0):
    """
    dim: head_dim (e.g., 128)
    max_position: target context length
    scale: extension factor (e.g., 8 means 8K → 64K)
    """
    # Original frequencies
    freqs = 1.0 / (base ** (torch.arange(0, dim, 2).float() / dim))
    
    # Ramp: 0 → 1 across dimensions
    t = torch.arange(dim // 2).float()
    ramp = t / (dim // 2 - 1)
    
    # NTK-aware scaled base
    new_base = base * scale ** (dim / (dim - 2))
    freqs_ntk = 1.0 / (new_base ** (torch.arange(0, dim, 2).float() / dim))
    
    # YaRN: interpolate per-dim based on ramp
    mixed = (1 - ramp) * freqs + ramp * freqs_ntk
    return mixed

# Position interpolation (simpler baseline)
def interpolate_positions(positions, old_max, new_max):
    ratio = old_max / new_max
    return positions * ratio  # index embedding at scaled position

# ponytail: YaRN also applies attention temperature scaling (sqrt(1/scale))
# and a fine-tuning step for best results

sections.title: PagedAttention & vLLM: OS-Style KV Cache Management
sections.description:
In production, serving multiple requests with different sequence lengths causes massive KV cache fragmentation — up to 60–80% VRAM wasted. PagedAttention (Kwon et al., 2023) borrows the OS virtual memory paging concept to fix this.

How it works: KV cache is split into fixed-size blocks (like memory pages). A block table maps logical token positions to physical blocks. Blocks can be non-contiguous in VRAM. New requests are allocated blocks on demand, returned when done.

Benefits:
• Near-zero internal fragmentation (only last block partially used)
• Copy-on-Write sharing for beam search (multiple beams share prefix blocks)
• Prefix caching: Blocks for a shared system prompt are reused across all requests → zero re-computation

vLLM implements PagedAttention and achieves 2–24× higher throughput vs HuggingFace naive serving.
sections.codeBlock:
# PagedAttention conceptual block table (simplified)
BLOCK_SIZE = 16  # tokens per block

class PagedKVCache:
    def __init__(self, n_layers, n_kv_heads, head_dim, total_blocks=1000):
        # Pre-allocate all physical KV blocks
        shape = (total_blocks, 2, n_kv_heads, BLOCK_SIZE, head_dim)
        self.store = torch.zeros(shape, dtype=torch.float16)
        self.free_blocks = list(range(total_blocks))

    def allocate(self, n_tokens):
        n_blocks = (n_tokens + BLOCK_SIZE - 1) // BLOCK_SIZE
        if len(self.free_blocks) < n_blocks:
            raise MemoryError('OOM: no free KV blocks')
        block_ids = [self.free_blocks.pop() for _ in range(n_blocks)]
        return block_ids  # logical → physical mapping

    def free(self, block_ids):
        self.free_blocks.extend(block_ids)  # return blocks to pool

# ponytail: real vLLM also handles CoW for beam search beam fork/merge

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• KV Cache Quantization — DeepSpeed inference: KV cache compression techniques
  • MQA: Fast Transformer Decoding — Multi-Query Attention: one KV head, significant cache reduction
  • GQA: Grouped-Query Attention — GQA paper by Ainslie et al. (Google), the standard for Llama/Mistral
  • Ring Attention with Blockwise Transformers — Distributed attention for context length scaling beyond single-GPU KV cache limits
  • CacheBlend: Fast LLM Inference by Augmenting KV Cache — Recent work on KV cache blending for multi-turn conversations

details:
Speculative Decoding: A technique to speed up auto-regressive generation: a small draft model generates K tokens in parallel, the large target model verifies them in one forward pass. If accepted, K tokens are generated for the cost of ~1. Works because verification is parallelizable (like pre-fill). Meta/Google use this in production. Speedup: 2–3× with no quality loss.

Quantized KV Cache: KV tensors can be quantized to INT8 or even INT4 independently of weights. Halves or quarters KV memory with minimal quality loss. Combined with GQA, this can reduce KV footprint by 16–32× vs naive MHA FP16.

Interview Cheat Sheet: Decode is memory-bandwidth bound (not compute). KV cache = 2 × L × H_kv × d_head × S × B × dtype. GQA reduces H_kv. PagedAttention eliminates fragmentation. FlashAttention reduces peak HBM usage. Speculative decoding adds throughput at no quality cost.
